radiology_order.exam: CT ABD & PELVIS W & W/O CONTRAST, ADDL SECTIONS

steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: biliary-duct obstruction/choledocholithiasis; pancreatic mass or ductal pathology (given steatorrhea-like stool changes, 30 lb weight gain, and family history of hemochromatosis/cirrhosis)
ensemble.information_gap: The etiology of the markedly elevated lipase (1059) is established as pancreatitis, but the underlying cause — specifically whether biliary pathology (choledocholithiasis, biliary sludge, or ductal dilation) is driving it — has not been imaged at this encounter; the prior RUQUS was negative but was performed at an outside facility at an unspecified prior time and may not reflect current anatomy.
ensemble.expected_finding: The physician is hoping to identify choledocholithiasis, biliary sludge, common bile duct dilation, or a pancreatic head mass/lesion that would explain the acute pancreatitis and the concerning stool changes (dark/light stools suggesting possible biliary obstruction or malabsorption).
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: With lipase >17× the upper limit of normal, confirmed acute pancreatitis, mildly elevated transaminases (ALT 55, AST 44), and stool changes suggestive of biliary obstruction or malabsorption, a right-upper-quadrant/abdominal ultrasound is the standard first-line imaging to identify a biliary etiology and assess the biliary tree and pancreatic head.
grounding: Lipase: 1059.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)
Amylase: 159.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0)
Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT): 55.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0)
Asparate Aminotransferase (AST): 44.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0)
History: 'sharp, burning pain in a band-like distribution in her epigastrium that radiated to her back'
History: 'BMs are more infrequent, smaller in volume, and can either be very dark or very light'
History: 'RUQUS that were negative for gallstones, sludge, or cholecystitis' (prior, not current encounter)
History: 'approx. 30 lb weight gain since ___'
Family History: 'Father died in ___. Had hemochromatosis, hepC, cirrhosis'
Physical exam: 'Soft, mildly-tender to palpation in epigastrium. Negative murphys sign. Abd distended but not-tympanitic.'
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has biochemically confirmed acute pancreatitis (lipase 1059, amylase 159) with mildly elevated transaminases and a history of recurrent episodes triggered by fatty foods, raising strong suspicion for a biliary etiology such as choledocholithiasis or biliary sludge not captured on a prior outside ultrasound. The stool changes (dark/light, variable volume) are concerning for intermittent biliary obstruction or pancreatic exocrine insufficiency, and the 30 lb weight gain with family history of hemochromatosis/cirrhosis adds further reason to evaluate the liver and biliary tree. A bedside abdominal ultrasound is the logical next step to assess for common bile duct dilation, choledocholithiasis, or hepatic/pancreatic structural abnormality that would explain the current episode and guide management.

vindication.actual_finding: The ultrasound showed no choledocholithiasis, no biliary sludge, no CBD dilation (CBD 3 mm), no gallbladder wall thickening or stones, and no pancreatic masses or ductal dilation.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The physician hoped to find biliary pathology or a pancreatic lesion explaining the acute pancreatitis and stool changes, but the study was entirely negative for all targeted findings.

steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS W & W/O CONTRAST, ADDL SECTIONS)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: pancreatic mass/malignancy, biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis not visible on US, intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN), or other pancreatic pathology given steatorrhea-like stool changes and significant weight gain
ensemble.information_gap: The ultrasound could not fully visualize the pancreas ('portions of the pancreatic tail obscured by overlying bowel gas'), leaving the extent and etiology of pancreatitis — and the possibility of an underlying pancreatic mass or ductal abnormality — uncharacterized
ensemble.expected_finding: Pancreatic edema/peripancreatic stranding confirming acute pancreatitis and its severity (Balthazar grade), and/or a pancreatic mass, ductal dilation, or obstructing lesion that could explain recurrent episodes, stool changes, and weight gain
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: With lipase >17× the upper limit of normal, an incomplete pancreatic view on ultrasound, recurrent episodes, stool character changes (dark/light, small volume), and unexplained 30-lb weight gain, CT with contrast is the standard next step to assess pancreatitis severity, identify complications (necrosis, pseudocyst), and evaluate for an underlying structural or neoplastic etiology.
grounding: Lipase: 1059.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)
Amylase: 159.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0)
History: 'sharp, burning pain in a band-like distribution in her epigastrium that radiated to her back'
History: 'similar episodes of pain in the past (beginning ___ year ago). This usually preceded ingestion of rich, fatty foods'
History: 'BMs are more infrequent, smaller in volume, and can either be very dark or very light'
History: 'approx. 30 lb weight gain since ___'
Prior imaging: 'portions of the pancreatic tail obscured by overlying bowel gas'
Prior imaging: 'There is no evidence of stones or gallbladder wall thickening'
Physical exam: 'Soft, mildly-tender to palpation in epigastrium. Negative murphys sign. Abd distended but not-tympanitic.'
ensemble.reasoning: The markedly elevated lipase (1059) with band-like epigastric pain radiating to the back confirms acute pancreatitis, but the ultrasound incompletely visualized the pancreatic tail and identified no gallstone etiology. Given recurrent episodes, stool changes suggestive of exocrine insufficiency or biliary dysfunction, and significant unexplained weight gain, I need CT with contrast to grade pancreatitis severity (necrosis, fluid collections), assess the full pancreatic parenchyma and duct, and exclude an underlying mass or structural lesion driving recurrent disease.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: pancreatic mass/malignancy, biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis not visible on US, intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN), or other pancreatic pathology given steatorrhea-like stool changes and significant weight gain
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The ultrasound could not fully visualize the pancreas ('portions of the pancreatic tail obscured by overlying bowel gas'), leaving the extent and etiology of pancreatitis — and the possibility of an underlying pancreatic mass or ductal abnormality — uncharacterized
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Pancreatic edema/peripancreatic stranding confirming acute pancreatitis and its severity (Balthazar grade), and/or a pancreatic mass, ductal dilation, or obstructing lesion that could explain recurrent episodes, stool changes, and weight gain
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: With lipase >17× the upper limit of normal, an incomplete pancreatic view on ultrasound, recurrent episodes, stool character changes (dark/light, small volume), and unexplained 30-lb weight gain, CT with contrast is the standard next step to assess pancreatitis severity, identify complications (necrosis, pseudocyst), and evaluate for an underlying structural or neoplastic etiology.

Amylase: 159.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0)

History: 'similar episodes of pain in the past (beginning ___ year ago). This usually preceded ingestion of rich, fatty foods'

Given recurrent episodes, stool changes suggestive of exocrine insufficiency or biliary dysfunction, and significant unexplained weight gain, I need CT with contrast to grade pancreatitis severity (necrosis, fluid collections), assess the full pancreatic parenchyma and duct, and exclude an underlying mass or structural lesion driving recurrent disease.

vindication.actual_finding: The pancreas enhances normally with no peripancreatic stranding, no fluid collections, no ductal dilation, no calcifications, and no pancreatic mass; a small pancreatic tail hypodensity is attributed to interdigitating fat.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The CT found none of the expected findings — no edema, stranding, Balthazar-gradable pancreatitis, mass, or ductal abnormality — directly contradicting the hypothesis that imaging would confirm active or structurally explained pancreatitis.
